brief:
The single most important signal this week is that nothing moved — and that itself demands our attention. Across the entire sovereign curve, from the **91-day at 9.85%** to the **15-year at 12.68%**, yields registered precisely zero basis points of change. In a market where inflation sits at **9.13%**, the repo rate is **10%**, and fiscal slippage is accelerating, a perfectly static curve is not equilibrium — it is a market waiting for a catalyst it hasn't yet found. The March 10 T-bill auctions cleared cleanly, with the 91-day drawing a healthy bid-cover of **1.92x** and the 364-day at **1.75x**, both with zero devolvement, confirming that the short end is well-bid and comfortable. But step out to the 10-year BGTB auction on March 8 and the picture shifts meaningfully: the bid-cover thinned to **1.4x** and Bangladesh Bank had to absorb **10% devolvement**. That is the market drawing a line — participants are happy to park liquidity in bills but are demanding a concession to extend duration, and even at **11.15%** on the 10-year, the concession was not enough to fill the book. The curve shape itself is telling: the **2-year at 11.95%** sits a full **80 basis points above the 10-year**, and the **15-year at 12.68%** towers above the 20-year's **11.65%** by over 100 basis points. This is not a normal term premium structure — it is a curve riddled with kinks that reflect fragmented demand, mark-to-market anxiety in bank portfolios, and the lingering distortions of an administered rate environment still finding its market-clearing levels.

The structural backdrop explains why this stasis may not last. Excess liquidity at **৳1.9 lakh crore** remains extraordinarily large, yet it is clearly not translating into duration appetite — banks are hoarding short and staying defensive. The call money rate at **9.80%** is hugging the repo floor, sitting just 20 basis points below the policy rate, which tells us interbank conditions are comfortable but not flush enough for anyone to feel compelled to reach for yield. The advance-to-deposit ratio at **75.8%** signals that credit growth remains subdued, consistent with an economy where real rates are barely positive and private sector confidence is still fragile. On the fiscal side, the numbers are sobering: revenue collection at just **44.9% of the FY26 target** with barely four months remaining virtually guarantees a surge in government borrowing in Q4. ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%** suggests spending will be back-loaded aggressively, adding to net supply just as the market is already reluctant on duration. The interest-to-revenue ratio at **22%** is a structural vulnerability that constrains fiscal flexibility and ensures the government remains a persistent, price-insensitive borrower. With total debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** — 40% of GDP — and M2 growth at **9.5%** running well below nominal GDP growth implied by current inflation, the monetary-fiscal tension is real and unresolved. Brent at **$78.4** and JKM LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu** provide no relief on the import bill, keeping pressure on the current account and limiting BB's room to ease even if growth falters.

For positioning, my call is clear and conviction is high. The short end — 91-day and 364-day bills — is fairly priced but offers no value beyond liquidity parking; at **9.85%** on the 91-day, you are earning negative real returns against **9.13%** CPI after tax. The belly of the curve is where the opportunity and the danger coexist. The **2-year at 11.95%** is anomalously cheap relative to the 5-year at **10.82%** — that 113 basis point inversion screams supply indigestion and forced selling, likely from banks managing SLR portfolios under mark-to-market stress. If you believe BB holds the repo rate at **10%** through H1 FY27, which I do, then selectively adding 2-year BGTBs at current levels offers roughly **200 basis points** of positive carry over the repo rate with manageable duration risk. I would avoid the 10-year entirely until devolvement clears — that **10% overhang** from the March 8 auction signals BB will need to sweeten the next offering, and patience will be rewarded with a better entry. The 15-year at **12.68%** looks optically attractive, but liquidity in that tenor is negligible and the mark-to-market risk into a Q4 supply deluge is asymmetric. The catalyst to watch is the next borrowing calendar release and any signal from BB on its Q4 auction sizes. If the government front-loads large bond issuance to close the revenue gap, the long end reprices **30-50 basis points wider** quickly, and the 2-year inversion corrects violently. Position for that.
